source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Compare and group materials together, according to whether they are solids, liquids or gases; observe that some materials change state when they are heated or cooled, and measure or research the temperature at which this happens in degrees Celsius.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack uses observable properties to classify states, then tracks reversible melting, freezing, evaporation and condensation while preserving the identity and amount of material.

objective.child_goal: I can classify solids, liquids and gases and explain how heating or cooling can change state.
objective.statement: Compare solids, liquids and gases by observable properties and explain common changes of state caused by heating or cooling.
misconceptions: Classifies soft, powdery or small-piece materials as liquids because they can be poured.
Thinks gases are weightless, are the same as air, or do not take up space.
Thinks melting makes a new substance or makes material disappear.
Uses evaporation and boiling as identical terms or thinks condensation is water leaking through a surface.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The form a material is in: solid, liquid or gas.
vocabulary.audio_script: A material's state can be solid, liquid or gas.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Three labelled observation trays for solid, liquid and gas.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A solid changing into a liquid when heated.
vocabulary.audio_script: Melting is the change from solid to liquid.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: An ice cube and liquid water joined by a heating arrow.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Liquid changing into gas at the surface.
vocabulary.audio_script: Evaporation happens when liquid changes into gas at its surface.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A lowering water line with an upward state-change arrow.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Gas changing into liquid when cooled.
vocabulary.audio_script: Condensation is the change from gas to liquid.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Water droplets forming on the outside of a cool container.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The unit we use to measure temperature.
vocabulary.audio_script: Temperature can be measured in degrees Celsius, written with a degree sign and C.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A thermometer scale labelled degrees Celsius.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Observe each sample: does it keep its own shape, flow to take the container's shape, or spread to fill the available space?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Activate property-based classification instead of relying on familiar object names.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Classify what the material does, not whether it feels hard or can be poured.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Safe virtual samples are tilted, compressed or transferred between transparent containers.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Solids keep their own shape, liquids flow and keep a fixed volume, and gases spread through the available space.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Build a comparative model from observable behaviour while allowing difficult cases such as powders.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A pile of grains can pour, but each grain remains a small solid with its own shape.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A comparison table records shape, flow and container behaviour for all three states.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Ice, liquid water and water vapour are the same material in different states. Heating or cooling changes the state.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model reversible state change without suggesting that material is created, destroyed or changed into a different substance.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The water changes state, but it is still water. Follow the arrows for melting, freezing, evaporation and condensation.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A closed water journey links solid, liquid and gas with labelled heating and cooling arrows and an unchanged amount marker.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Change the temperature, observe the state, record degrees Celsius and name the change.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Connect measured temperature, observation and state-change vocabulary.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Move the temperature in safe steps. Record what you observe before naming the change.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A simulation shows a sample, thermometer, observation table and heating/cooling controls.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: A puddle becomes smaller on a warm day. Has the water vanished, soaked through the tray, or changed into a gas?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Detect disappearance and evaporation/boiling misconceptions using evidence.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The liquid water changes state at its surface and mixes with the air as water vapour.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A water-level graph and covered-container comparison show material transfer rather than disappearance.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion why droplets form on the outside of a cold can even when the can does not leak.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Use explanation evidence to connect cooling, water vapour and condensation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Where was the water before the droplets appeared, and what change of state happened?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A sealed cold can sits on a dry card while droplets form only on the cool outer surface.

manipulatives.purpose: Compare materials using repeatable tests for shape, flow and occupation of container space.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners choose a sample, run tilt/transfer/compress tests, record observations and classify only after evidence is visible.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Each test has a text description and still before/after frames; keyboard buttons replace drag or tilt gestures; containers have patterned outlines as well as colour; a results table is announced row by row; and no rapid pressure, splash or vapour animation is used.

manipulatives.purpose: Link temperature in degrees Celsius to observed state and named changes while conserving material.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners adjust temperature with safe virtual controls, pause at observation points and add records to a table.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Arrow keys change temperature by one degree and Page Up/Down by ten; the current temperature, direction of change and observed state are announced; reduced-motion mode uses discrete still states; and every graph has a data-table equivalent.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave classification with state-change explanations after property language is secure; use water-cycle examples only after evaporation and condensation are distinguished.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Classify familiar and tricky materials from observed properties rather than surface appearance.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: material sample + two or three virtual property tests + solid/liquid/gas classification + evidence selection
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include powders, grains, gels and inflated containers carefully; teacher review must confirm the intended model is age-appropriate and not overgeneralised.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict and name melting, freezing, evaporation and condensation from heating or cooling.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: starting material/state + heating or cooling direction + observed end state + named change + same-material check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use substances and temperatures supported by the item data; do not imply every material changes state at water's temperatures.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Read degrees Celsius, record observations and identify the interval in which a state change occurs.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: thermometer or table + ordered observations + identify state/change + cautious conclusion
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Distinguish measured evidence from researched reference values and avoid false precision from coarse observations.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain everyday evaporation and condensation using observations and state-change vocabulary.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: everyday scenario + evidence panel + competing explanations + because response
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include puddles, washing and cold surfaces; avoid unsafe heating instructions and distinguish evaporation from boiling.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain classification and state-change vocabulary through short mixed retrieval.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: review queue alternates property, change-name and evidence items with prior-error weighting
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use static presentation by default for repeated classroom retrieval and include a text-only equivalent.

body.prompt: Sand can be poured. Which observation best shows that sand is made of solids?
choices: Each grain keeps its own shape
The whole pile takes the exact shape of every container
Sand spreads to fill all available space

expected_answer.value: Each grain keeps its own shape

hints: Look closely at one grain rather than the whole pile.
Pouring alone does not make a material a liquid.
question_variants.explanation: Each grain of sand is a small solid that keeps its own shape, even though many grains can flow past one another when poured.

body.prompt: An ice cube is heated until it becomes liquid water. Name the change and decide whether it is still the same material.

hints: A solid changing to a liquid is called melting.
Only the state changes; the material remains water.
question_variants.explanation: Heating causes the solid ice to melt into liquid water. It is the same material in a different state.

body.prompt: Two equal puddles are left in trays. The warm tray's puddle becomes smaller first. Which explanation fits the evidence?
observations: The warm tray loses liquid water faster
choices: More liquid changed into water vapour from the warmer puddle
The warm tray destroyed the water
The water boiled because all evaporation is boiling

expected_answer.value: More liquid changed into water vapour from the warmer puddle

hints: Both trays are dry underneath, so the water did not leak through.
Evaporation can happen below boiling temperature.
question_variants.explanation: The warmer puddle evaporates faster: more liquid water changes into water vapour at the surface, even though it does not boil.

body.prompt: Droplets form outside a sealed cold can standing on dry card. Explain where the droplets come from.
observations: The card beneath stays dry at first
Droplets form on the cold outer surface

expected_answer.value: Water vapour in the air cools at the can's surface and condenses into liquid droplets.

hints: The sealed can is not leaking.
Think about water as a gas in the surrounding air.
question_variants.explanation: Water vapour in the air is cooled by the can and changes from gas to liquid. This change is condensation.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Classifies soft, powdery or small-piece materials as liquids because they can be poured.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner uses can be poured as the only evidence and classifies sand, rice or beads as liquid.
repair_sequence: Zoom in from the pile to one piece or grain.
Test whether each piece keeps its own shape.
Compare many moving solids with a liquid that forms one continuous level surface.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner classifies a new granular sample as many small solids and cites shape evidence.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks gases are weightless, are the same as air, or do not take up space.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner describes an apparently empty container as containing nothing or says gas cannot fill a space.
repair_sequence: Compare a deflated and inflated sealed bag in the virtual lab.
Observe that the inflated bag occupies more space, resists being squashed and changes a sensitive virtual balance reading.
Name the particular gas when known and distinguish gas as a state from air as a mixture.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains that gas occupies space and has mass, and identifies air as a mixture containing gases.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks melting makes a new substance or makes material disappear.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner labels liquid water after melting as a different material or records less material without evidence.
repair_sequence: Track the material name before and after heating.
Use a closed-container or unchanged-amount marker.
Cool the liquid to reverse the change and name freezing.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner describes melting and freezing as reversible state changes of the same material.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses evaporation and boiling as identical terms or thinks condensation is water leaking through a surface.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner requires bubbles for evaporation or says droplets outside a sealed container came through its wall.
repair_sequence: Compare slow surface evaporation below boiling with boiling throughout a liquid.
Use a sealed cold-surface observation to rule out leaking.
Trace liquid-to-gas and gas-to-liquid arrows and cite the observed evidence.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner distinguishes evaporation from boiling and identifies surrounding water vapour as the source of condensation.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use quiet laboratory scenes, no bubbling audio by default, no sudden vapour clouds and one observation panel at a time.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace pouring, spreading and state-change motion with labelled before/after stills and a user-controlled next-step button.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Announce sample, test, temperature and observation in that order; provide replay without adding facts not visible in the evidence.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use icons beside solid, liquid, gas, heating and cooling; chunk explanations; and provide a glossary without revealing answers.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Lock unused lab controls, show OBSERVE-CLASSIFY-EXPLAIN progress and pause the simulation automatically at each evidence point.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, allow temperature changes in single steps, preserve the observation table and provide undo/reset without penalty.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Treat surprising samples as scientist challenges, keep correct observations during reclassification and reward evidence updates.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach state, solid, liquid, gas, melt, freeze, evaporate, condense, heat, cool and degrees Celsius with paired diagrams and sentence frames.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires property-based classification and correct naming of common heating/cooling changes in at least two formats. Secure requires explanation from recorded evidence, same-material reasoning and accurate distinction of evaporation, boiling and condensation on two retained sessions.
evidence.parent_summary: Can compare solids, liquids and gases and explain how heating or cooling changes materials between states.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Separates observations, classification, temperature records and explanations so teachers can see whether scientific vocabulary is supported by evidence.
next_actions: Route pourable-material errors to grain-level property testing.
Route disappearance claims to closed-system and same-material tracking.
Connect evaporation and condensation to the water cycle once both changes are independently secure.
Schedule mixed property and state-change retrieval at 1, 3, 7, 14 and 30 days.

qa.notes: Deep review pack. Human review should verify tricky-material classifications, substance-specific temperatures, safe virtual-investigation wording, graph alternatives and all accepted explanations before promotion.
